meta.title: Naming Decline after Temporal Lobe Surgery
meta.outcome: Predicted probability of clinically relevant postoperative naming decline (a drop of ≥5 raw points on the 60-item Boston Naming Test) in adults undergoing temporal lobe resection for pharmacoresistant epilepsy, from 5 easily obtained preoperative predictors.
meta.cff_info:
What this shows. This calculator reproduces the primary nomogram of Busch et al. (Neurology 2018) estimating the probability of clinically relevant naming decline — a decline of ≥5 raw points on the Boston Naming Test (BNT) — after temporal lobe resection (TLR) for drug-resistant temporal lobe epilepsy. It uses five preoperative variables that are routinely available in most epilepsy surgery centers: side of resection, age at epilepsy onset, age at surgery, sex, and years of education.

How to read it

  • The result is the percent chance of a ≥5-point BNT decline (0–100) after surgery.
  • Dominant-hemisphere (typically left) resection is by far the strongest driver — it raises the odds of decline roughly 14-fold; naming decline after nondominant resection is uncommon.
  • Older age at epilepsy onset and older age at surgery increase the estimated risk; more years of education slightly decrease it; female sex nudges it upward.

Key findings

  • Developed on 719 patients (Cleveland Clinic) and externally validated on 138 patients from 3 US centers; 26% of the development cohort had clinically relevant naming decline.
  • Discrimination was excellent — c-statistic 0.83 in development and 0.81 in external validation, with excellent calibration.
  • Dominant-sided resection (OR 14.07, 95% CI 8.48–23.34) and each year older at epilepsy onset (OR 1.05, 95% CI 1.03–1.07) were the significant predictors; age at surgery, sex, and education were retained to sharpen discrimination.
  • Worked nomogram example: onset age 15, dominant resection, age 60 at surgery, female, 10 years education → ≈58% predicted risk (this calculator returns 58.4%).

A separate second model (side of surgery, age at epilepsy onset, preoperative BNT score) predicts moderate-to-severe decline (≥11 points; c = 0.84) and is not implemented here.

Demonstration and counseling aid — not a substitute for a multidisciplinary epilepsy surgery evaluation.

predictors.name: Side of resection
predictors.assess: Language-dominant vs nondominant temporal lobe resection, determined by fMRI or Wada testing (in right-handers, left = dominant). Dominant-sided resection is the strongest predictor of naming decline (β = 2.644; OR 14.07).

predictors.name: Age at epilepsy onset
predictors.assess: Age (years) at onset of epilepsy. Later onset predicts greater risk of naming decline — each additional year raises the odds ~5% (β = 0.048; OR 1.05). Development-cohort median 14 years.

predictors.name: Age at surgery
predictors.assess: Age (years) at the time of temporal lobe resection. Older age at surgery modestly increases risk (β = 0.015; OR 1.02, not independently significant but improves discrimination). Development-cohort mean 36 years.

predictors.name: Sex
predictors.assess: Patient sex. Female sex is associated with a slightly higher estimated risk (β = 0.367; OR 1.44, not independently significant but retained to improve discrimination).

predictors.name: Years of education
predictors.assess: Total years of formal education. More education is associated with a slightly lower estimated risk of naming decline (β = −0.049; OR 0.95, not independently significant). Development-cohort mean ~13 years.

model.panel_title: Chance of clinically relevant naming decline
model.note: Percent chance of a clinically relevant (≥5-point BNT) naming decline after temporal lobe resection. Dominant-sided resection is the dominant driver; nondominant resections rarely cause decline. Busch et al., Neurology 2018 (c-statistic 0.81–0.83).
model.viz_info: Each predictor contributes its published logistic weight (β × value for the age/education sliders; the side and sex options add their β when the higher-risk category is chosen). The weights plus the model intercept (−3.802) are summed and passed through a logistic function to give the percent chance of naming decline. The waterfall shows how each factor pushes the estimate up or down; the donut shows the resulting probability.

provenance.source: Busch 2018 Neurology Table 3 (published logistic coefficients + intercept, clinically-relevant ≥5-point model) and Figure 3 nomogram (worked example) — PMID 30404781, doi 10.1212/WNL.0000000000006629.
provenance.notes: Logistic model, ≥5-point BNT decline: LP = -3.802 + 0.048·onset_age + 2.644·[dominant] + 0.015·age_surgery + 0.367·[female] - 0.049·education; pct = 100/(1+exp(-LP)). Coefficients & intercept from Table 3. Verified vs Figure 3 worked example (onset 15, dominant, age 60, female, education 10 → LP 0.339 → 58.4%, paper ≈58%). c-statistic 0.83 (development) / 0.81 (external validation). Second model (≥11-point moderate-severe decline: intercept -6.545, onset 0.045, dominant 2.772, baseline BNT 0.03; c=0.84) not implemented. Extracted 2026-07-08.
